agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation in a logistics/courier context. A user (sender or possibly receiver) wants to cancel a delivery order. Cancellation eligibility is tightly coupled to the physical reality of the fulfillment pipeline — once a courier has picked up a parcel or it is in transit, cancellation has fundamentally different cost and operational implications than cancelling a newly placed order.","bounded_context":"Order Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A request for courier/delivery service, tracking a parcel from sender to recipient through its lifecycle."},{"term":"Order Status","definition":"The current state of an order in its lifecycle (e.g., Placed, Confirmed, Assigned, Picked Up, In Transit, Out for Delivery, Delivered, Cancelled)."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of terminating an order before its successful completion (delivery). Cancellation eligibility and consequences depend on order status."},{"term":"Cancellation Window","definition":"The set of order statuses during which cancellation is permitted without extraordinary intervention."},{"term":"Cancellation Fee","definition":"A charge applied when cancellation occurs after resources (courier, vehicle, fuel) have already been committed."},{"term":"Refund","definition":"The return of payment to the user upon successful cancellation, potentially reduced by a cancellation fee."},{"term":"Sender","definition":"The user who placed the order and is the primary party authorized to cancel."},{"term":"Courier","definition":"The assigned driver/rider responsible for pickup and delivery of the parcel."},{"term":"Pickup","definition":"The physical act of a courier collecting the parcel from the sender. A critical state boundary — pre-pickup vs post-pickup cancellation are fundamentally different operations."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order may only be cancelled if its current status is within the cancellation window (e.g., Placed, Confirmed, Assigned). Orders that have been Delivered or are already Cancelled cannot be cancelled.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Allowing cancellation of a delivered order would corrupt order state — the physical parcel has already changed hands. Allowing double-cancellation corrupts financial records.","testable_assertion":"Given an order with status Delivered or Cancelled, when a cancellation is attempted, then the system must reject it with an appropriate error."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Only the sender (order creator) or an authorized system actor (e.g., admin, support agent) may cancel an order. The recipient cannot cancel.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Allowing unauthorized parties to cancel orders violates data integrity and opens the door to fraud and denial-of-service against senders.","testable_assertion":"Given a cancellation request from a user who is not the sender and not an admin, the system must reject the request with a 403/unauthorized error."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Cancellation of an order that has been Assigned to a courier must notify the courier that the assignment is void.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If the courier is not notified, they will attempt pickup, wasting resources and creating a broken state where the courier holds a task the system considers cancelled.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in Assigned status that is cancelled, then a cancellation notification event must be emitted targeting the assigned courier."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"When an order is cancelled after a courier has been assigned or after pickup, a cancellation fee may be applied based on the stage of fulfillment.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Fee amounts and thresholds are business policy decisions that may change with market conditions, promotions, or executive decisions.","testable_assertion":"Given an order cancelled in Assigned status, the refund amount equals the original charge minus the applicable cancellation fee for that stage."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"A cancelled order must transition to Cancelled status atomically — partial cancellation states must not be observable.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If cancellation is partially applied (e.g., status updated but refund not initiated, or courier not notified), the system is in a corrupt, inconsistent state.","testable_assertion":"Given a cancellation request, either the order status changes to Cancelled AND the refund is initiated AND the courier is notified (if assigned), or none of these occur."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"A refund must be initiated for every successful cancellation. The refund amount depends on cancellation fee policy.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Taking payment for a service not rendered without issuing a refund is a financial integrity violation and potential regulatory/legal issue.","testable_assertion":"Given a successful cancellation, a refund record must be created with amount >= 0 linked to the original order payment."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"An order that has been Picked Up (parcel is physically with courier) requires a return-to-sender flow before cancellation completes. Cancellation is allowed but enters a 'Cancellation Pending' state until the parcel is returned.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"The parcel is a physical object — you cannot cancel it out of existence. The system state must reflect physical reality: the courier has the parcel and must return it.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in Picked Up or In Transit status that is cancelled, the order transitions to Cancellation Pending, and a return-to-sender task is created for the courier."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order exists with status Placed, Confirmed, or Assigned","when":"The sender requests cancellation","then":"The order status transitions to Cancelled, a refund is initiated for the full amount minus any applicable cancellation fee, and a cancellation confirmation is returned to the user."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order exists with status Picked Up or In Transit","when":"The sender requests cancellation","then":"The order status transitions to Cancellation Pending, the assigned courier is notified to return the parcel to sender, and the user is informed that cancellation is in progress pending parcel return."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An order in Cancellation Pending status and the courier confirms parcel return","when":"The return is recorded in the system","then":"The order transitions from Cancellation Pending to Cancelled, and a refund minus the applicable cancellation fee is initiated."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An order exists with status Delivered or Cancelled","when":"The sender requests cancellation","then":"The system rejects the request with a clear error message indicating the order cannot be cancelled in its current state."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A user who is NOT the sender of the order","when":"They request cancellation of that order","then":"The system rejects the request with an authorization error."},{"id":"AC-6","given":"An order in Assigned status","when":"The sender cancels the order","then":"The assigned courier receives a notification that the assignment has been voided before they attempt pickup."},{"id":"AC-7","given":"An order exists with status Placed (no courier assigned, no fee incurred)","when":"The sender requests cancellation","then":"A full refund is initiated with zero cancellation fee."},{"id":"AC-8","given":"A cancellation request is submitted but the refund service or courier notification fails mid-process","when":"The system detects a partial failure","then":"The entire cancellation is rolled back, the order remains in its original status, and the user is informed to retry. No partial state is persisted."},{"id":"AC-9","given":"An order in Out for Delivery status (courier is en route to recipient)","when":"The sender requests cancellation","then":"The order transitions to Cancellation Pending, the courier is instructed to abort delivery and return the parcel, and the highest-tier cancellation fee applies."},{"id":"AC-10","given":"Two simultaneous cancellation requests for the same order","when":"Both requests are processed","then":"Exactly one cancellation succeeds and exactly one refund is created. The second request receives an error indicating the order is already cancelled or cancellation is already in progress."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Cancellation requested at the exact moment the courier marks the order as Picked Up (race condition between status transition and cancellation).","expected_behavior":"The system must resolve the race deterministically. If pickup is recorded first, the order enters Cancellation Pending with a return-to-sender flow. If cancellation wins, the courier is notified the order is void before they depart. Optimistic locking or version checks on order status should prevent split-brain."},{"scenario":"Courier becomes unreachable after a post-pickup cancellation is initiated (Cancellation Pending but courier cannot be notified).","expected_behavior":"The order remains in Cancellation Pending. The system retries courier notification with backoff. After a threshold, the case is escalated to operations/support. The parcel is not considered returned until explicitly confirmed — no automatic timeout to Cancelled."},{"scenario":"Payment provider is down when refund needs to be processed.","expected_behavior":"The cancellation is recorded but the refund is queued for retry. The user is informed that the cancellation is confirmed but the refund is being processed. The system must not block cancellation on refund success — these are eventually consistent but the cancellation state itself is committed."},{"scenario":"User cancels an order, then the order status update event from the courier (e.g., Picked Up) arrives late due to network delay.","expected_behavior":"The system must reject stale status transitions on an already-Cancelled order. The courier's late Picked Up event must not override the Cancelled status. Event ordering must be enforced via order version or timestamp."},{"scenario":"Sender requests cancellation of an order that is being split or consolidated with other orders in a batch delivery.","expected_behavior":"Only the specific order is cancelled. The remaining orders in the batch are unaffected. The courier is notified of the updated manifest. If the cancelled order was the only order in the batch, the entire batch assignment is voided."},{"scenario":"Free/promotional order is cancelled — no payment was made.","expected_behavior":"Cancellation proceeds normally. Refund amount is zero. No cancellation fee is charged (fee cannot exceed original payment). The Cancelled status is still recorded."},{"scenario":"User cancels and immediately places a new identical order.","expected_behavior":"The new order is treated as a completely independent order with its own lifecycle. No implicit linking or deduplication. The cancellation of the first order proceeds independently."},{"scenario":"Admin/support agent cancels on behalf of the sender.","expected_behavior":"The cancellation follows the same rules but the audit trail records the acting admin, the reason, and that it was an override. The sender is notified that their order has been cancelled."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Cancellation fees recover partial costs of committed resources (courier time, fuel). Without proper cancellation fee logic, the business absorbs the full cost of wasted dispatches. Refund accuracy is critical — over-refunding erodes margin, under-refunding creates disputes and chargebacks.","operational":"Post-pickup cancellations create return-to-sender logistics overhead. High cancellation rates in Assigned/Picked Up states waste courier capacity and reduce fleet utilization. Operations teams need visibility into cancellation-pending orders to manage courier workloads.","user":"Users expect a simple, fast cancellation experience for orders not yet in motion. Transparency about cancellation fees and the return process for in-progress orders builds trust. Poor cancellation UX (unclear errors, stuck states) is a top driver of support tickets and churn.","risk":"Race conditions between cancellation and status updates can create ghost orders (cancelled but courier still delivers) or orphaned refunds. Lack of idempotency on cancellation can cause double refunds. Post-pickup cancellation without a return flow means lost parcels.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Senders (order creators)","impact":"Directly affected — they initiate cancellation and receive refunds. Their experience defines satisfaction with this feature."},{"group":"Couriers","impact":"Must receive timely void/return notifications. Unnotified cancellations waste their time and erode trust in the platform."},{"group":"Recipients","impact":"Indirectly affected — a cancelled order means expected delivery will not arrive. No direct action required, but notification may be appropriate for confirmed orders."},{"group":"Finance/Accounting","impact":"Refund accuracy, cancellation fee reconciliation, and revenue recognition all depend on correct cancellation state management."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will handle edge cases (stuck Cancellation Pending, disputed fees, late cancellations). Need tooling to view cancellation state and override when necessary."},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch","impact":"Need visibility into cancellation rates by stage to optimize courier assignment timing and reduce wasted dispatches."}]},"confidence":{"level":42,"reasoning":"The spec is a single sentence — 'Users should be able to cancel their order.' It provides no definition of order lifecycle states, cancellation window policy, fee structure, authorization model, or post-pickup handling. All business rules above are inferred from standard logistics domain knowledge, not from an explicit specification. Multiple critical policy decisions (fee amounts, which statuses allow cancellation, whether recipients can cancel, post-pickup flow) are unspecified and could vary significantly by business model.","high_confidence_areas":["Delivered and already-Cancelled orders cannot be cancelled (physical reality)","Only authorized parties can cancel (security invariant)","Assigned courier must be notified on cancellation (operational necessity)","Cancellation must be atomic — no partial states (data integrity)"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact cancellation fee policy and amounts per stage","Whether 'In Transit' and 'Out for Delivery' orders are cancellable at all vs hard-blocked","Whether recipients should be notified of cancellations","Whether Cancellation Pending is a real domain status or handled differently","Refund timing guarantees and retry policy"],"assumptions":["The order lifecycle follows standard logistics stages: Placed → Confirmed → Assigned → Picked Up → In Transit → Out for Delivery → Delivered","Cancellation fees are a business concept in this system (not all courier platforms charge them)","Sender is the primary authorized cancellation actor","Post-pickup cancellation requires a physical return-to-sender flow","The system supports refunds (i.e., payment is collected upfront, not cash-on-delivery only)"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec does not define which order statuses permit cancellation. Is cancellation allowed after pickup? After the courier is en route? The business impact varies enormously — blocking post-pickup cancellation is simpler but may frustrate users; allowing it creates complex return logistics.","affected_stakeholders":["Senders","Couriers","Operations"],"options":["A: Allow cancellation only pre-pickup (Placed, Confirmed, Assigned). Simple, low operational cost.","B: Allow cancellation at any pre-delivery stage with a return-to-sender flow for post-pickup. Complex but user-friendly.","C: Allow cancellation at any stage but charge escalating fees. Balanced approach."],"recommendation":"Option C — escalating fees aligned with committed resources. This is the industry standard for mature courier platforms."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec does not define the cancellation fee structure — flat fee, percentage, or stage-dependent. This is a core business policy that affects revenue and user experience.","affected_stakeholders":["Senders","Finance/Accounting"],"options":["A: No cancellation fees (full refund always)","B: Flat cancellation fee after courier assignment","C: Tiered fees increasing with fulfillment progress (e.g., 0% pre-assignment, 20% post-assignment, 50% post-pickup, 80% out-for-delivery)"],"recommendation":"Option C — tiered fees proportional to resources consumed. Requires product/finance alignment on exact percentages."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec says 'users' but does not clarify whether the recipient of a delivery can also cancel, or only the sender. In some courier models (e.g., food delivery), the recipient IS the orderer.","affected_stakeholders":["Senders","Recipients"],"options":["A: Only the sender (order creator) can cancel","B: Both sender and recipient can cancel","C: Depends on the order type / business model"],"recommendation":"Option A for now — only the order creator. Recipient cancellation is a separate feature with different authorization and refund implications."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No order lifecycle state machine is defined in the spec. The acceptance criteria above assume standard logistics states. The actual system may use different terminology or states.","affected_stakeholders":["All engineering and product stakeholders"],"options":["Review existing codebase for order status enum/state machine","Request a state diagram from product"],"recommendation":"Review existing codebase to ground the acceptance criteria in the actual ubiquitous language before implementation begins."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

Cancellation eligibility is tightly coupled to the physical reality of the fulfillment pipeline — once a courier has picked up a parcel or it is in transit, cancellation has fundamentally different cost and operational implications than cancelling a newly placed order.","bounded_context":"Order Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term"
